title: The Girl Who Loved Wild Horses
author: Paul Goble

content.text: Long ago, in a land of wide plains and towering mountains, there lived a Native American girl who loved wild horses more than anything in the world.
She understood them in a way no one else did—how they moved, how they breathed, how their ears twitched when they were curious or afraid.
The people of her village admired her gift, for she could walk among the wild herds without fear, and the horses would nuzzle her hands as if she were one of their own.
One summer, when the grass was tall and golden, the girl’s people decided to capture some of the wild horses to tame and train.
They set out on a great roundup, driving the herds toward a hidden corral.
The girl rode with them, her heart both excited and uneasy.
She loved the wild horses, but she also loved her people and wanted to help them.
As the herd thundered across the plain, the girl’s horse stumbled on a gopher hole.
She was thrown to the ground, and before anyone could reach her, the wild horses swept past like a storm, their hooves kicking up dust.
When the dust settled, the girl was gone.
The people searched and searched, but she had vanished as if the earth had swallowed her whole.
Days passed, and the girl’s family mourned her, believing she had been trampled or lost forever.
But the girl was not lost.
She had been lifted onto the back of a great stallion, a leader of the wild herd, and carried far away to a hidden valley where the grass was sweet and the water ran clear.
The horses welcomed her as one of their own, and she lived among them, running free beneath the endless sky.
At first, the girl missed her family terribly.
She would stand on the highest ridge and look toward the distant campfires of her people, her heart aching.
But as time passed, she grew to love her new life.
She learned the language of the wind and the whispers of the grass.
She raced with the young colts and rested in the shade with the mares.
The stallion who had saved her became her protector, and she became the heart of the herd.
Years went by, and the girl’s people never forgot her.
Sometimes, when the wind blew just right, they would hear the distant sound of hooves and wonder if it was her.
Then one day, a terrible storm swept across the plains.
Lightning split the sky, and the wild horses, frightened and scattered, galloped toward the village.
The people ran to their lodges, but the girl—now grown into a young woman—stood her ground.
She raised her hands and called to the horses in their own language.
One by one, they slowed, their ears pricked forward, listening.
The great stallion stepped forward and bowed his head to her, and the herd followed him away from the village, back to the safety of the hills.
The people watched in amazement. They recognized her at once—their lost girl, now wild and free.
They called out to her, begging her to come home, but she only smiled and shook her head.
She belonged to the horses now, and they belonged to her.
With a final wave, she turned and ran with the herd, vanishing into the golden light of the setting sun.
From that day on, the people of the village would sometimes see a young woman riding at the head of the wild herd, her hair streaming like a banner in the wind.
And if you listen closely on a quiet evening, when the stars are bright and the air is still, you might hear the sound of hooves in the distance—the girl and her horses, running free forever.
